coredata.dc:description: The photocatalytic carbon dioxide reduction reaction (CO2RR) is an innovative and reliable technology to address the issues of energy shortage and climate change simultaneously. However, issues such as insufficient light absorption, rapid electron-hole recombination and low surface activity during CO2RR severely limit the catalysts’ efficiency for photocatalysis. In this study, we present nanohybrid N-CDs/FeNbO4 catalysts to overcome these challenges and achieve high-efficient photocatalytic conversion of CO2 to CH4. The enhancement process starts with the introduction of modified N-CDs, which have a broad light absorption capability. These N-CDs also help to modulate the interfacial charge transfer kinetics, leading to improved charge separation and transfer. This, in turn, enhances the multielectron transfer conversion selectivity of CO2 to CH4. Meanwhile, CO2 adsorption on the photocatalysts is increased due to the creation of oxygen vacancies and lattice stresses resulting from the introduction of N-CDs. This work presents a new approach for improving the photocatalytic performance by adjusting surface structure and interfacial behaviors.

head.ce:title: N-doped carbon dots-modulated interfacial charge transfer and surface structure in FeNbO4 photocatalysts for enhanced CO2 conversion selectivity to CH4

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We utilized the functionalization of N-CDs to systematically modulate the structure and electron transfer behavior of FeNbO4 catalysts for enhanced CO2 conversion selectivity to CH4 in an aqueous solution without any sacrificial agent. This approach shows promise in creating an efficient photocatalyst for the reduction of CO2, allowing for tunable CH4 product selectivity.

The photocatalytic carbon dioxide reduction reaction (CO2RR) is an innovative and reliable technology to address the issues of energy shortage and climate change simultaneously. However, issues such as insufficient light absorption, rapid electron-hole recombination and low surface activity during CO2RR severely limit the catalysts’ efficiency for photocatalysis. In this study, we present nanohybrid N-CDs/FeNbO4 catalysts to overcome these challenges and achieve high-efficient photocatalytic conversion of CO2 to CH4. The enhancement process starts with the introduction of modified N-CDs, which have a broad light absorption capability. These N-CDs also help to modulate the interfacial charge transfer kinetics, leading to improved charge separation and transfer. This, in turn, enhances the multielectron transfer conversion selectivity of CO2 to CH4. Meanwhile, CO2 adsorption on the photocatalysts is increased due to the creation of oxygen vacancies and lattice stresses resulting from the introduction of N-CDs. This work presents a new approach for improving the photocatalytic performance by adjusting surface structure and interfacial behaviors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The use of sustainable solar energy has drawn a lot of attention worldwide due to the increasing utilization of fossil fuels and related environmental issues of greenhouse gases and global warming [1–4]. In fact, the CO2 emissions have increased by almost 30 %, over the last 60 years, which intensely provoking the issues of global warming and climate change. Therefore, converting greenhouse CO2 into other valuable chemicals by utilizing renewable solar energy has become a hotly debated topic in the scientific community. The photocatalytic conversion of CO2 to hydrocarbon fuels under sunlight is a significant approach to alleviate the environmental and energy crisis [5–9].

The CO2 photoreduction process comprises intricate and sequential multi-electronic and light-assisted chemical reactions between atmospheric carbon dioxide and water on the catalyst surface. When the photocatalyst is exposed to photons with sufficient energy (equal to or greater than its band gap energy), the electron-hole pairs may generate in the conduction and valance band respectively. The surface of the catalyst plays a crucial role in adsorbing CO2 and water molecules for the redox reaction, which ultimately produces valuable fuels such as CO, CH4, etc [10]. However, there are still many challenges regarding the development of a catalyst for photocatalytic reduction of CO2 such as wide band gap, limited light response range, rapid recombination of electrons and holes, weak absorption and photoactivation of photocatalyst as well as the low yield and poor selectivity of CO2 reduction reaction (CO2RR) products.

A large number of researchers have reported the modification of catalysts by structural and composition fabrication to solve the aforementioned problems [11–15]. Many strategies have been used to upgrade the photocatalyst performance, including element doping, band heterojunction, incorporation of other functional materials as co-catalysts, and conjunction with other semiconductors [16–18]. Semiconductors' valence band position may be effectively tuned to narrow their band gap by introducing doping elements like N and C in metal oxide [19,20]. Heterostructures may also be used to provide excellent photocatalytic activity. Heterostructures are usually composed of two or more semiconductors with different energy band structures and unique interfacial sites to promote charge carrier separation [21]. In addition, supported cocatalyst is considered to be one of the most promising methods, which can simultaneously achieve accelerated charge separation and accelerated surface reaction kinetics [22].

To address the issues in photocatalytic CO2 reduction, carbon dots (CDs) with unique photochemical and photophysical properties have been developed to enhance the performance of traditional semiconductor-based photocatalysts [23,24]. CDs have abundant functional groups (–OH/–COOH, etc.) on their surface, which can be effectively combined with semiconductor materials to form a stable assembly structure. N-CDs absorb light over a wider range of wavelengths, which exhibit photoluminescence and can act as light-harvesting components due to their broad absorbance in the visible range. The presence of nitrogen-containing functional groups (such as amines) on N-CDs enhances CO2 adsorption, a key step in the reduction process. In addition, the intramolecular hydrogen bonding of CO and COOH groups on the CDs caused strong upward band bending and made it easier for the photocarriers to separate and stabilize [25]. The introduction of nitrogen atoms into the CDs structure improves charge separation and reduces electron-hole recombination, which is crucial for enhancing photocatalytic efficiency [26–28]. Thus, N-CDs acting as co-catalysts can enhance the photoinduced electron transport and electron storage properties, provide highly active and selective sites to modulate the adsorption and activation of CO2, and further boost the catalytic efficiency [29,30].

The metal oxide FeNbO4 is a non-toxic and harmless substance promising as a photocatalyst in the CO2 reduction reaction. It possesses a narrow band gap and a wide light absorption range. The surface structure and interface design of FeNbO4 are important for improving performance. The surface engineering can modify the surface reactive sites and promote the reaction. The interface design with fast charge transfer rate can reduce the interfacial recombination losses, and contribute to the sluggish kinetics of multielectron transfer. In this study, we utilized the functionalization of CDs to systematically modulate the structure and electron transfer behavior of FeNbO4 catalysts using a simple microwave irradiation method. The resulting N-CDs/FeNbO4 composite demonstrated high-efficiency photoconversion of CO2 to CH4 (18.681 μmol g−1h−1) in an aqueous solution without the need for any sacrificial agent. This represents a 2.77-fold increase compared to pristine FeNbO4 (6.752 μmol g−1h−1) and a 5.41-fold increase compared to N-CDs (3.453 μmol g−1h−1), respectively. The surface structure of FeNbO4 is modulated by the introduction of N-CDs, which leads to the creation of oxygen vacancies and lattice stresses, ultimately enhancing CO2 adsorption. Through transient photovoltage (TPV) tests, it is demonstrated that the N-CDs modulate the interface behavior of FeNbO4, resulting in a faster electron transfer rate, and then enhancing the CO2 conversion selectivity to CH4. This study presents a novel approach to improving photocatalytic performance by adjusting surface structures and interfacial behaviors.

All the chemicals, including 99 % pure Niobium (V) pentachloride (NbCl5) and Ferric (III) chloride (FeCl3), ethanol, ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH), citric acid, ethylenediamine and barium sulfate (BaSO4) were analytical grade agents (Sigma Aldrich) and were used as received. The other chemicals and solvents used in the experiment were of reagent grade and utilized without additional purification.

The FeNbO4 catalyst was prepared through the microwave method with some modifications in the reported procedure [31]. First, 5 mmol of NbCl5 (1.3 g) was dissolved in 10 mL of absolute ethanol, and 5 mmol of FeCl3 (811 mg) was dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water. The solutions were mixed with continuous stirring, and the pH of the reaction mixture was adjusted to ∼10 using ammonium hydroxide. The mixture was then transferred to a round bottom flask and subjected to a 500 W microwave reactor for 20 min. After cooling to room temperature, the contents were centrifuged to obtain reddish-brown precipitates, which were washed thoroughly with deionized water and ethanol to remove any excess unbound metal salts. The sample was dried in an oven at 60 °C for 24 h, followed by calcination at 800 °C for 2 h.

The N-CDs were synthesized by following the reported hydrothermal method and subsequently freeze-drying treatment [32,33]. 11 mmol of citric acid (2.10 g) and 10 mmol of ethylenediamine (670 µL) were dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water and stirred for five minutes to obtain a homogenous solution. The resultant clear solution was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave (HTG-50, Anhui Chem-n Instrument Co., Ltd) and heated at 180 °C for 5 h. After cooling to ambient temperature, the resulting reaction mixture was freeze-dried under vacuum to obtain N-CDs as a dark brown solid.

The N-CDs/FeNbO4 nanohybrid was synthesized by ultrasonic treatment following the hydrothermal method. For this, 11 mmol of citric acid (2.10 g) and 10 mmol of ethylenediamine (670 μL) were separately dissolved in distilled water (10 mL). Then, both solutions were mixed and stirred to give a homogenous solution. Afterward, 25 mg of FeNbO4 was added to the above stirring solution and sonicated for 1 h. After sonication, the resulting solution was transferred into a stainless-steel Teflon-lined autoclave, heated at 180 °C for 5 h, cooled, centrifuged to obtain a brown solid, rinsed with deionized water, and dried at 60 °C for 24 h. Seven N-CDs/FeNbO4 nanohybrid samples were prepared by varying the amount of FeNbO4 (25, 35, 50, 55, 60, 75 and 100 mg) following the above procedure. The samples were labeled as N-CDs/FeNbO4-25, N-CDs/FeNbO4-35, N-CDs/FeNbO4-50, N-CDs/FeNbO4-55 (labeled as N-CDs/FeNbO4), N-CDs/FeNbO4-60, N-CDs/FeNbO4-75 and N-CDs/FeNbO4-100, respectively. During optimization of the above-prepared catalysts, the N-CDs/FeNbO4nanohybrid was identified as the most effective for CO2 reduction.

The photocatalytic activity of prepared N-CDs/FeNbO4 nanohybrid was investigated in CO2RR. Initially, 20 mg of the N-CDs/FeNbO4 was dispersed in 20 mL of deionized water and placed in the high-pressure photocatalytic reactor (NSV Plus100, Anhui Kemi Machinery Co., Ltd.). The reactor was deaerated and then purged by CO2 to saturate its internal environment for 10 cycles, and finally, the pressure of CO2 was set to 2 MPa in the closed reactor. The reaction mixture in the reactor was illuminated by a 300 W Xenon lamp (PLS-SXE 300C (BF), Perfect Light, China) from the topside of the reactor. However, the system was calibrated by vigorously stirring the reaction mixture in the dark for 30 min before the irradiation for the photocatalytic reaction. It must be mentioned that the reactor's solution was constantly stirred during the radiation exposure. After 2.5 h of exposure to radiation, we collected gaseous samples from the reactor and performed composition analysis using a Shimadzu GC 2014 Gas Chromatography system. This system was equipped with a MolSieve 5A and a PoraPLOT Q column, as well as a thermal conductivity detector (TCD) and a flame ionization detector (FID). The experimental setup for the photocatalytic reaction is illustrated in the supporting information (Fig. S1).

The FeNbO4 was synthesized through the microwave irradiation method (Fig. 1 a, Experimental section 2.3), and N-CDs were grown in situ in FeNbO4 by hydrothermal method [31], with a detailed description provided in the Experimental Section. The surface morphologies and internal structure of the FeNbO4 and N-CDs/FeNbO4 nanohybrid were characterized by SEM and TEM. The SEM image of FeNbO4 and N-CDs/FeNbO4 showed clear granules of FeNbO4 with dimensions of about 100 nm in diameter. Since N-CDs were very fine and microscopic particles, therefore, the carbon dots could not be spotted by SEM (Fig. S2a and c). However, carbon dots can be Figured out by TEM and HRTEM [34]. The TEM image of N-CDs is presented in Fig. 1b, these circled positions indicate that N-CDs already exist. Additionally, we can infer the size and morphology of the N-CDs through their photoluminescence (PL) characteristics. According to the literature, smaller N-CDs typically emit at shorter wavelengths (in the blue region), while larger N-CDs may emit at longer wavelengths (green or red regions). The fact that our N-CDs exhibit blue emission suggests that their size is likely very small (Fig. S3). Fig. 1c is the HRTEM image of N-CDs/FeNbO4 which enables atomic-scale imaging of a catalyst crystallographic structure. The lattice spacing of 0.308 nm corresponds to the {111} facets of FeNbO4, while the lattice spacing of 0.218 nm corresponds to the {100} planes of N-CDs [35–37]. EDX analysis (Fig. S4) and mapping analysis (Fig. 1(d–i)) affirmed that N-CDs/FeNbO4 photocatalysts are composed of Fe, Nb, O, C, and N and also verified the homogenous dispersion of N-CDs onto FeNbO4 nanocomposites surface [33,38].

X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was carried out to characterize and determine the phases of the resulting composite photocatalyst. In Fig. 2 a, the XRD pattern of N-CDs/FeNbO4 of nanohybrid is shown. The distinct diffraction peaks observed at 2θ = 24.8°, 30.6°, 35.8°, 36.6°, 53.7°, 54.3°, and 65.1°, correspond to the (011), (111), (200), (120), (202), (122), and (231) facets of the orthorhombic FeNbO4 crystal (PDF#16-0358), respectively. In the composite XRD pattern, there were some additional peaks, which were caused by the anchoring of N-CDs onto the surface of FeNbO4. However, the peaks of CDs were not very intense due to their low content and amorphous structure[39,40]. Furthermore, the diffraction peak of N-CDs/FeNbO4 shifts slightly to the left compared to pure FeNbO4 (Fig. 2b), which also indicates that the lattice structure of FeNbO4 remains intact by the addition of N-CDs [41].

FTIR spectroscopy was performed to examine the surface functional groups and chemical composition of N-CDs/FeNbO4 (Fig. S5). Peaks observed at 1050 and 1550 cm−1 were attributed to CNH and CN (amide linkage), while the peaks at 1660 and 3260 cm−1 were assigned to the CO and OH bond stretching vibrations, respectively [34]. The FT-IR spectrum of N-CDs indicates the presence of abundant hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino functional groups. Raman spectroscopy was used for further examination of the FeNbO4 and N-CDs/FeNbO4 structure. As depicted in Fig. S6, the spectrum displays two distinct peaks at 287 cm−1 and 353 cm−1, which are typically linked to the bending vibration modes within the FeNbO4 structure. These peaks likely correspond to the bending of NbO bonds or the vibrations of FeO bonds. Additionally, a significant peak at 590 cm−1 is observed, representing the symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations of NbO bonds. Another peak, located at 802 cm−1, is indicative of the stretching vibrations of NbONb bonds. Two distinct peaks observed near 1383 and 1577 cm−1 in the N-CDs/FeNbO4 composite correspond to the D band attributed to sp3 hybridized carbon and the G band resulting from the in-plane vibration of sp2 hybridized carbon. They respectively reflect the defects and impurities present in the carbon dots, as well as the degree of order in the graphene layer, further indicating the successful introduction of CDs [42].

XPS analysis was performed to identify surface elements' composition and electronic states of N-CDs/FeNbO4. In Fig. 2c, the XPS survey spectrum confirmed the presence of Fe, Nb, O, C, and N elements in the N-CDs/FeNbO4 composite. Fig. 2d presents the XPS spectra of Fe 2p, revealing two characteristic peaks at binding energies of 711.61 eV (Fe 2p3/2) and 725.42 eV (Fe 2p1/2), along with satellite peaks at 718.03 eV and 733.3 eV, respectively. This indicates that Fe in the N-CDs/FeNbO4 composite catalyst mainly exists in the form of Fe3+ [43]. In the Nb 3d XPS spectra (Fig. 2e), the binding energies of Nb 3d5/2 and Nb 3d3/2 were around 207.07 and 209.8 eV, respectively [44]. As shown in Fig. 2f, the O1s spectrum shows three peaks representing O2− ions at the lattice region at 529.8 eV binding energy, oxygen-deficient regions at 531.2 eV binding energy, and surface oxygen at 532.6 eV binding energy, respectively. In Fig. 2g, the peaks at 284.3, 285.1, 286.2, and 288.1 eV correspond to CC, CN, CO, and CO, respectively [45,46]. In Fig. 2h, on the curve of N 1s, the peaks at 398.7, 400.2, and 402.6 eV are regarded as Pyridinic N, Pyrrolid N, and Graphitic N, respectively. The above XPS spectrum results showed the cooccurrence of FeNbO4 and N-CDs in the composite photocatalyst. In addition, we show in Fig. S7 that the XPS spectrum of pure FeNbO4 also contains oxygen vacancies. The presence of oxygen vacancies (VO defects) in the catalyst was further confirmed through electron spin resonance (ESR), as illustrated in Fig. 2i. Both pure FeNbO4 and the N-CDs/FeNbO4 composite exhibited ESR signals with a g value of 2.003, which is characteristic of oxygen vacancies [47]. Notably, the introduction of N-CDs into the FeNbO4 matrix enhanced the ESR signal corresponding to oxygen vacancies.

UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) was conducted to investigate the optical properties and band structure of FeNbO4, N-CDs, and N-CDs/FeNbO4. The addition of N-CDs induces a red shift in the UV–Vis spectrum, enhancing FeNbO4′s light absorption in the visible light region (Fig. 3 a). As the N-CDs/FeNbO4 nanohybrid showed good absorbance in the visible region therefore could be used as a promising material in light-driven photocatalytic reactions. Furthermore, the Tauc plot was computed using Eq. (1):

where ɑ represents the absorbance index, h denotes the Planck constant, v stands for the frequency, A is a constant, and Eg signifies the band gap. The calculated curves are depicted in Fig. 3b. The Mott-Schottky (M−S) experiment was utilized to assess the conduction band position and determine the semiconductor type of FeNbO4, N-CDs, and the N-CDs/FeNbO4 composite samples. As illustrated in Fig. S8, the positive slopes exhibited by FeNbO4 and N-CDs indicate that the synthesized materials possess n-type semiconducting properties. The flat band potential of FeNbO4 is −0.6 V (relative to the Ag/AgCl electrode). In the case of n-type semiconductors, the conduction band position of FeNbO4, N-CDs, and N-CDs/FeNbO4 is approximately 0.1 V lower than the flat band potential. The ECB of N-CDs and FeNbO4 can be calculated by using this Eq. (2):

The Eg of FeNbO4 and N-CDs/FeNbO4 were calculated as 1.96 eV and 1.81 eV respectively, suggesting that the presence of N-CDs can decrease the bandgap of FeNbO4 (Fig. 3c). Under Xe lamp light irradiation, a large Eg would restrict photon-generated carriers, while a narrow Eg promotes the easy excitation of charge carriers [48].

To understand the effect of the separation efficiency of photo-generated charges in N-CDs/FeNbO4 samples on photocatalytic CO2 reduction activity, the steady-state photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the catalytic system were studied (Fig. S9). After combining N-CDs with FeNbO4, the fluorescence decreased, confirming the charge or energy transfer between the composite systems, indicating better efficiency in separating photo-generated electron-hole pairs. The dynamics of photo-excited charge carrier transfer were further studied through time-resolved PL decay spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 3d, the fluorescence decay curve closely conforms to the double exponential equation, and the average lifetime is determined using Eq. (3):

τ represents the average lifetime of photo-generated charge carriers. The fluorescence lifetime of N-CDs/FeNbO4 (13.8289 ns) is much longer than that of FeNbO4 (1.7637 ns), indicating that the recombination of photo-generated e−-h+ in N-CDs/FeNbO4 is greatly suppressed. This also indicates that N-CDs/FeNbO4 have a higher photogenerated charge transfer efficiency. Therefore, we can infer that the improvement of photocatalytic activity is due to the significant effect of introducing N-CDs into FeNbO4 on the separation efficiency of photo-generated carriers, which are important factors in the photocatalytic CO2 reduction reaction.

As shown in Fig. 3e, N-CDs/FeNbO4 possessed a higher photocurrent response than both pure FeNbO4 and N-CDs, exhibiting the boosted charge carriers transfer by the introduction of N-CDs in FeNbO4. The transient photocurrent analysis verified the separation of photogenerated charge carriers on the surface of the N-CDs/FeNbO4 and thus enhanced the photocatalytic reduction reaction of CO2. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) analysis was performed to characterize the samples and explore the effect of N-CDs on CO2 photoreduction activity, as illustrated in Fig. 3f. Comparing three different electrodes (FeNbO4/FTO, N-CDs/FTO, and N-CDs/FeNbO4/FTO), the N-CDs/FeNbO4/FTO electrode showed the smallest Nyquist semicircular diameter. The reduced charge transfer resistance (Rct) observed in the N-CDs/FeNbO4 composite (Rct = 4 Ω) compared to pure FeNbO4 (Rct = 8 Ω) and N-CDs (Rct = 10 Ω) alone suggests that the incorporation of N-CDs effectively facilitates interface charge exchange between N-CDs and the metal oxide [47]. This improvement in interfacial charge transfer likely strengthens the bonding between the N-CDs and FeNbO4, resulting in enhanced charge transfer efficiency. Consequently, this synergistic effect significantly boosts the photocatalytic activity of the composite, enabling faster and more efficient charge separation and transfer during the photocatalytic process [49].

The photocatalytic activity of N-CDs/FeNbO4 for the CO2 reduction into hydrocarbon compounds, most notably CH4 was investigated in detail, and compared the efficiency of the prepared catalyst with the FeNbO4 [50]. The reduction reaction was carried out in the aqueous phase without a sacrificial agent under simulated Xe lamp light, followed by the analysis of the gaseous products using gas chromatography (GC). Each catalyst was tested three times in parallel to reduce chance factors. The results showed that the main gaseous products of N-CDs/FeNbO4 were CH4 and CO. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the CH4 yield of N-CDs/FeNbO4-55 catalyst (labeled as N-CDs/FeNbO4) is 18.681 μmol g−1h−1, which is 2.77 times and 5.41 times higher than that of pristine FeNbO4 (6.752 μmol g−1h−1) and N-CDs (3.453 μmol g−1h−1), respectively. Without photocatalysts, without water, or under Ar atmosphere, no products were generated (Fig. 4b). Only a very small amount of CH4 and CO gas was produced with the catalyst in the CO2 atmosphere under dark conditions. These control experiments under varied conditions validated that the products detected genuinely resulted from the photocatalytic reaction of CO2 and H2O facilitated by the catalyst. Fig. 4c shows the yield of CH4 and CO for FeNbO4 with different ratios of N-CDs and FeNbO4. When the amount of FeNbO4 is 55 mg, the photocatalyst achieves the highest CH4 yield. It is worth noting that compared pure FeNbO4, with the introduction of N-CDs can effectively promote the CO2 reduction reaction toward CH4 production, rather than the production of CO [40]. In addition, we conducted a comparative experiment with a mixture of N-CDs and FeNbO4 to investigate the differences in their photocatalytic performance and N-CDs/FeNbO4 nanohybrid performance (Fig. S10). The experimental results indicate that the N-CDs/FeNbO4 nanohybrid materials, N-CDs can be uniformly dispersed on the surface of FeNbO4, providing a larger contact area and more active sites to promote photocatalytic reactions. However, in the physical mixture, due to the uneven distribution of N-CDs and FeNbO4, the contact area is reduced, and active sites may be blocked, leading to lower photocatalytic efficiency.

Nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy was used to analyze the liquid phase products. As shown in Fig. 4d, the peaks at chemical shifts of 4.8 and 2.74 are D2O and DMSO, respectively. FeNbO4 and N-CDs/FeNbO4 both showed trace ethanol peaks at chemical shifts of 1.1 and 3.6, but these can be disregarded for liquid-phase products. For further examination of stability, the N-CDs/FeNbO4 catalyst was repeatedly exposed to new reaction media for CO2 photoreduction reactions. This is illustrated in Fig. 4e, the N-CDs/FeNbO4 catalyst did not show a significant decrease in photocatalytic activity after undergoing five consecutive cycles, and the CH4 yield still reached 90.7 % of the initial sample. The SEM, XRD, and EDS characterization of N-CDs/FeNbO4 photocatalyst after continuous photocatalytic reaction are shown in Fig. S2, Fig. S11, and Fig. S12. The results indicate that after long-term photocatalytic reactions, the nanoparticle structure, crystal phase, and elemental composition of N-CDs/FeNbO4 photocatalyst remain unchanged, demonstrating its excellent stability and reusability throughout the entire photocatalytic process.

To further explore the key factors influencing photocatalytic performance through the introduction of N-CDs. The behavior of interfacial electron transmission at the FeNbO4 photocatalyst with and without N-CDs was examined using the previously described stimuli-responses TPV approach (see the experimental details in the supporting information and Fig. S13). Electrons on the catalyst's surface were excited by the laser stimulation source (355 nm). According to the typical TPV decay curves (Fig. S14a–c), the signal decay times for FeNbO4, N-CDs, and N-CDs/FeNbO4 were 1.56 ms, 4.15 ms, and 2.22 ms, respectively. It could be seen that the electron-hole pair recombination rate was lowered in the synthesized nanohybrid photocatalyst because it took longer time for the electrons of N-CDs/FeNbO4 to revert to the ground state than it did for the original FeNbO4.

In order to further investigate the dynamics of electron transfer, the average electron relaxation time (τ) was determined by fitting the TPV decay curve. As presented in Fig. S14d, the τ value of N-CDs/FeNbO4 (0.2552 ms) was greater than that of FeNbO4 (0.2449 ms) and N-CDs (0.2459 ms), revealing the superior photocatalytic performance of N-CDs/FeNbO4 for charges extraction compared to that of FeNbO4. Fig. S15a and b shows the tmax and the integral area (S), where tmax indicates the time required for the curves to rise from the baseline to the apex, and S represents the maximum amount of excited charge for FeNbO4 and N-CDs/FeNbO4. The tmax values were 0.0974 ms and 0.1679 ms, respectively, for N-CDs/FeNbO4 and FeNbO4, demonstrating faster electron extraction in N-CDs/FeNbO4. The S values for N-CDs/FeNbO4 and FeNbO4 were found to be 0.054 and 0.017 respectively, illustrating that N-CDs anchored onto the surface of FeNbO4 effectively promote the maximum amount of photo-generated charge. The charge transfer rate for FeNbO4 and N-CDs/FeNbO4 was determined by dividing the integral area by the tmax value, and they were 0.1013 ms and 0.5544 ms, respectively. The interaction between the Fe-Nb ions and the functional groups on the N-CDs was responsible for the increase in charge transfer rate.

The interface electron transfer kinetics of N-CDs/FeNbO4 were examined through the application of Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) and Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT) techniques, which converted the intensity-time curve into an amplitude-frequency curve for detailed analysis of the TPV curves. The result of FFT on the N-CDs/FeNbO4 TPV relaxation curve (red curve in Fig. S14c) was shown in Fig. 5 a. The monotonic decrease in the amplitude of vibration suggests that the original TPV relaxation curves did not exhibit any significant static or periodic frequency components [51]. Therefore, the electron transfer process could be studied more conveniently by the TPV electron attenuation signal. CWT was used to examine the oscillating signal in the time–frequency domain. The 3D CWT TPV results are displayed in Fig. S16 and the transformed time–frequency-intensity plots are illustrated in Fig. 5b. In addition to the TPV curves of N-CDs/FeNbO4, the TPV decay curves of FeNbO4 and N-CDs were also subjected to analysis using FFT and CWT (Fig. S17a-b and S18a-b). Furthermore, the three-dimensional representations of the time–frequency-amplitude plots for FeNbO4 and N-CDs are provided in the supporting information (Fig. S17c and S18c), offering a comprehensive visualization of the data.

According to frequency-intensity curves, as shown in Fig. 5c, the strong peaks at high and low frequencies could be seen in the pink curve at t = 0.10 ms, suggesting that both the slow and fast electron transfer processes have a significant impact on electron transfer at this time. However, at 0.50 ms, the high-frequency peak almost disappears at 350 Hz as can be visualized from the purple curve. Therefore, as the time proceeds from 0.01 ms to 1.0 ms, the peaks were more concentrated in the low-frequency range (0–150 Hz) and the slow electron transmission mechanism was the most important route in the overall electron delivery process.

According to the time-intensity curve (Fig. 5d), when the frequency was less than 5 Hz (f = 1 Hz, 3 Hz), the position of the wavelet peak was restricted in the region of t < 4.5 ms, which indicated that the slow electron transmission process occurred in the long-time range throughout the electron delivery process, while as the frequency increased above 5 Hz, the accelerated electron delivery processes mainly occurred in the beginning time 0–2 ms stage of the electron transfer process. The dominance of electrons in the low-frequency region points to the strong electron transport capability of the N-CDs/FeNbO4 nanohybrid. Similarly, we also made a series of analyses on FeNbO4 and N-CDs, and the results (Fig. S17d–e and Fig. S18d–e) were similar to those of N-CDs/FeNbO4.

The positions of the peaks distributing over the timescale for the N-CDs/FeNbO4 together with those of pristine FeNbO4 and N-CDs at 50 Hz are shown in Fig. 5e for comparison. A slight delay in peak position in the intensity-time curve affirmed that the N-CDs have a significant contribution to the accumulation and release of photogenerated electrons, and N-CDs/FeNbO4 is more beneficial to the electronic transmission process. Three sets of peak time periods for FeNbO4, N-CDs, and N-CDs/FeNbO4 were measured at the same frequency within the 10–300 Hz range and the average delay time of the peak relative to N-CDs/FeNbO4 was calculated (Δt, Fig. 5f). This demonstrates that the FeNbO4 and N-CDs gave comparable results. The delay time (Δt) was found to be less than 0 at 10–100 Hz and almost equal to 0 at 100–300 Hz, indicating that N-CDs have accelerated the charge transfer processes [52]. Therefore, the N-CDs could speed up the process of extracting electrons from the interface in the nanohybrid of N-CDs/FeNbO4. Consequently, N-CDs/FeNbO4 are conducive to the multi-electrons reduction reaction and effectively improve the selectivity of CH4 products.

To further investigate the effect of N-CDs on the surface structure of FeNbO4, strain distributions were investigated by HRTEM and inverse fast Fourier transform (IFFT) patterns (Fig. 6 ). The dislocations and lattice strains of FeNbO4 and N-CDs/FeNbO4 were evidenced by HRTEM (Fig. 6a and f), the corresponding IFFT patterns (Fig. 6b and g) and geometric phase analysis (Fig. 6c–e, h–j). During the composite process, the introduction of N-CDs may undergo chemical reactions or interact with the surface of FeNbO4, leading to changes in the local structure or crystal defects in FeNbO4, which in turn affects its lattice structure [53]. These results demonstrated that the lattice strains were generated in FeNbO4 and N-CDs/FeNbO4. The lattice stress in N-CDs/FeNbO4 is more significant, possibly because anchoring of CDs onto the surface of FeNbO4 increased the density of dislocation lattice stripes at the interface. In certain cases, lattice strain can enhance the catalyst's active sites, improve the adsorption capacity of surface reactants, and facilitate the conversion of reactants, ultimately leading to better catalytic performance [53].

The first-principles calculation was carried out as well. It was interesting that the N-CDs did not bring a significant change of CO2 to the original catalyst, but when oxygen vacancies were introduced, the role of N-CDs became obvious. From the calculation results (Fig. 7 a–d), it can be seen that in terms of energy changes, the addition of N-CDs or the introduction of oxygen vacancies on the surface of FeNbO4 did not significantly change the adsorption of CO2 [54,55]. However, when there were oxygen vacancies, FeNbO4 with N-CDs showed much better adsorption capacity for CO2 than FeNbO4 without N-CDs. Considering that during the annealing process, there must be oxygen vacancies existing due to the reduction of carbon, and oxygen atoms will be lost in the form of CO2 or CO, which can be verified from the XPS and ESR results. CO2-TPD analysis is used to examine the materials' surface basicity (Fig. S19). Three desorption peaks can be identified in the CO2-TPD curves, corresponding to CO2 desorption from a weak basic site (surface hydroxyl groups) at 50–200 °C, medium basic site (metal–oxygen pairs) at 200–400 °C, and a strong basic site (low coordination oxygen atoms) at 400–550 °C. The examined catalysts demonstrated that the interaction of N-CDs and FeNbO4 has improved the significant abundance of moderate basic sites on the catalyst surface following the improvement of CO2 adsorption on the catalyst surface for the reduction reaction [25,56]. Therefore, the oxygen vacancies and N-CDs are supposed to coexist at the same time, whose synergetic effect has greatly enhanced the adsorption capacity of FeNbO4 for CO2 and the subsequent photocatalytic performance [57,58].

In order to further investigate the reaction mechanism of CO2 and H2O on N-CDs/FeNbO4, we utilized in-situ Diffuse Reflectance Infrared Fourier Transform Spectroscopy (DRIFTS) (See Fig. 8 ). This technique is a potent method for obtaining localized information about reaction species and their intermediates, which were thus applied on the pristine FeNbO4 (Fig. 8a) and N-CDs (Fig. 8b), N-CDs/FeNbO4 (Fig. 8c–e), respectively. It was shown in Fig. 8d that the intermediates began to appear gradually as the duration of light exposure increased. Some infrared peaks at 1235 and 1291 cm−1 were assigned to the b-CO3 2− and peaks at 1390 cm−1 were identified for HCO3 − ions. More importantly, the absorption bands at 1551 and 1566 cm−1 gradually increased which could be attributed to the *COOH group, while another peak at 2080 cm−1 could be ascribed to the characteristic bands of *CO in Fig. 8e. Notably, the *COOH group is a key intermediate for the photoreduction of CO2 to CH4 or CO. In addition, as shown in Fig. 8c there were also some infrared peaks of *CHO and *CH3O groups in the range of 1000–1200 cm−1. It was noteworthy that the peak intensity of *COOH steadily increased as the time of illumination increased ensuring photocatalytic CO2RR into CH4 [59–61]. On the other hand, the CO* absorption band on N-CDs/FeNbO4 is notably less intense compared to that on FeNbO4 and N-CDs. This suggests that CO* may undergo rapid protonation to form CHO* rather than being desorbed from the catalyst surface to produce CO molecules. This could potentially enhance the selectivity of the product in light-driven CO2 reduction to CH4. During the photocatalytic CO2 reduction, the catalyst activates and dissociates H2O molecules, yielding protons that facilitate the reduction of CO2 [62]. Concurrently, these protons consume holes, effectively suppressing the recombination of electrons and holes. In the current reaction system, almost no O2 production has been observed, and we can use the iodometric method to detect the generation of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) (Fig.S20). The productivity of H2O2 was measured to be 18.01754 μmol g−1h−1 using an aqueous solution of N-CD/FeNbO4 catalyst after photocatalytic CO2 reduction reaction as the test solution [63]. The rate is different from the theoretical yield, attributed to the decomposition of H2O2 during the photocatalytic process [64]. Additionally, the detection of hydroxyl radicals (▪OH) on the surface of the N-CDs/FeNbO4 catalyst was confirmed using the DMPO capture agent in the ESR (Fig. S21). Under illumination, the ▪OH signal was found to be stronger compared to that observed in the absence of light, indicating the role of N-CDs in enhancing the photocatalytic activity by increasing the concentration of reactive oxygen species. Based on the reaction intermediates and CO2 photoreduction activity of N-CDs/FeNbO4, the suggested reaction pathway for CO2 photoreduction on N-CDs/FeNbO4 could be outlined as follows (Fig. 8f): CO2 → COOH* → CO* (Partially, CO↑) → CHO* → CH2O* → CH3O* → CH3*→ CH4↑, where the asterisks indicate catalytic active sites and the vertical arrows depict the gas released during the reaction. In addition, the introduction of N-CDs significantly accelerates the protonation of key intermediates (especially CO*→CHO*), thereby enhancing the selectivity toward CH4.

Based on these results, the CO2 photocatalytic reduction process of N-CDs/FeNbO4 was supposed and illustrated in Fig. S22. Light irradiation can excite the electrons in VB of FeNbO4 and drive them to leap from VB to CB. The excited electrons in the CB were rapidly transferred to the N-CDs at the surface of the catalyst resulting in the separation of charge carriers. The excited electron captured by CDs reacted with surface-adsorbed CO2 to give methane while the holes in the VB of FeNbO4 were filled by water molecules to produce oxygen molecules and proton donors [65]. The introduction of N-CDs also modulates the surface structure of FeNbO4 and leads to the creation of oxygen vacancies and lattice stresses, ultimately enhancing CO2 adsorption. Furthermore, the presence of functional groups (e.g., –OH, C=O, –COOH) on the surface of CDs significantly enhances the electron transfer and mitigates electron-hole recombination, thereby enhancing the photocatalytic activity and selectivity of the catalyst [66].

In summary, we designed N-CDs/FeNbO4 nanohybrid photocatalysts through a novel microwave-assisted method. The obtained photocatalyst showed remarkable efficiency in selective photocatalytic reduction of CO2 into CH4 (18.681 μmol g−1h−1) under simulated light irradiation in pure aqueous solution without any sacrificial agent. As compared to pristine FeNbO4, the nanohybrid demonstrated a 2.77-fold increase in photocatalytic activity yield and the electronic selectivity of CH4 reached 91.83 %. The combination of experimental analyses and theoretical simulation demonstrated that the accelerated charge kinetics and surface structure modulation are attributed to the induction of N-CDs. First-principles calculation shows that the introduced oxygen vacancies boosted the absorbance of CO2. Furthermore, the TPV result indicated that FeNbO4 photocatalysts modified with N-CDs could produce a high amount of charge promptly due to a faster rate of charge migration, which is responsible for the increased photocatalytic activity and selectivity under light irradiation. In-situ DRIFTS demonstrated that the introduction of N-CDs significantly facilitated the generation of the key intermediates CHO*, which is crucial to the formation of CH4. The modulation of interfacial charge transfer and surface structure provides a new avenue for designing and applying multicomponent semiconductor-based catalysts in photocatalytic CO2 reduction and related systems with tunable product selectivity.
